title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: In 2022, the relationship between economic output and energy efficiency across nations shows a wide dispersion, with energy intensity measured in kilowatt-hours per dollar of GDP plotted against GDP per capita on a logarithmic scale. The chart uses a color-coded legend to distinguish regions, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania, revealing that high energy intensity does not strictly correlate with low income, nor does high wealth guarantee low energy usage per unit of output. At the upper extreme of energy intensity, Turkmenistan, North Korea, Venezuela, and Bahrain all consume more than 4 kilowatt-hours per dollar, standing well above the majority of the dataset. Conversely, countries with the lowest energy intensity, such as Denmark, Norway, and Ireland, cluster near the bottom of the vertical axis with values below 1 kilowatt-hour per dollar, despite occupying the far right of the horizontal axis where GDP per capita exceeds $50,000. A dense grouping of African nations, including Burundi, Liberia, Chad, and Mozambique, appears in the lower-left quadrant, characterized by GDP per capita below $2,000 and energy intensity generally under 2 kilowatt-hours per dollar. Intermediate positions are held by countries like Russia, Canada, and Australia, which show high GDP per capita but maintain energy intensity levels between 1 and 2.5 kilowatt-hours per dollar. The distribution lacks a single linear trend, as nations with similar income levels display vastly different energy profiles; for instance, Qatar and Kuwait sit at the high-income end with intensity above 1.5, while neighboring Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates show lower intensity. The data points show that while many wealthy European nations achieve low energy intensity, resource-rich economies in the Middle East and Central Asia maintain high energy consumption relative to their economic output.
